content.text:
{A just balance and scales are the Lord's; all the weights in the bag are his work.}{Proverbs 16:11}
 {Mathematics is the art of giving the same name to different things.}{— Henri Poincaré}

Linear systems appear throughout science, engineering, and economics. 
In this section, we explore several applications that demonstrate the 
power of the techniques we have developed.

Many real-world situations involve the flow of materials, traffic, or 
information through a network of nodes and connections. Conservation 
laws require that flow into each node equals flow out.

content.title: Traffic flow
content.solution:
At each node, flow in equals flow out:
$$
\text{Node } A&: 500 + x_4 = x_1 \\
\text{Node } B&: x_1 = 300 + x_2 \\
\text{Node } C&: x_2 + 400 = x_3 \\
\text{Node } D&: x_3 = 600 + x_4
$$
Rearranging and row reducing:
\[
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & 0 & -1 & 500 \\
1 & -1 & 0 & 0 & 300 \\
0 & 1 & -1 & 0 & -400 \\
0 & 0 & 1 & -1 & 600
\end{bmatrix}
\to
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & 0 & -1 & 500 \\
0 & 1 & 0 & -1 & 200 \\
0 & 0 & 1 & -1 & 600 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
\]
With $x_4 = t$ as the free parameter:
\[
x_1 = 500 + t, \quad x_2 = 200 + t, \quad x_3 = 600 + t, \quad x_4 = t
\]
Since traffic flows must be nonnegative, we need $t \geq 0$. Thus there 
are infinitely many solutions, with minimum flows when $t = 0$.

content.text:
Chemical reactions must conserve atoms of each element. Finding the 
correct stoichiometric coefficients is a linear algebra problem.

content.title: Combustion of propane
content.problem:
Balance the equation for the combustion of propane:
\[
x_1 \,\text{C}_3\text{H}_8 + x_2\,\text{O}_2 \to 
x_3\,\text{CO}_2 + x_4\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
\]
content.solution:
Conservation of each element gives:
$$
\text{Carbon}&: 3x_1 = x_3 \\
\text{Hydrogen}&: 8x_1 = 2x_4 \\
\text{Oxygen}&: 2x_2 = 2x_3 + x_4
$$
Moving all terms to one side:
\[
\begin{bmatrix}
3 & 0 & -1 & 0 \\
8 & 0 & 0 & -2 \\
0 & 2 & -2 & -1
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \\ x_4 \end{bmatrix} = 
\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
\]
Row reducing:
\[
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & 0 & -1/4 \\
0 & 1 & 0 & -5/4 \\
0 & 0 & 1 & -3/4
\end{bmatrix}
\]
With $x_4 = 4t$: $x_1 = t$, $x_2 = 5t$, $x_3 = 3t$, $x_4 = 4t$.

Taking $t = 1$ gives the balanced equation:
\[
\text{C}_3\text{H}_8 + 5\,\text{O}_2 \to 3\,\text{CO}_2 + 4\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
\]

content.text:
The economist Wassily Leontief won the Nobel Prize for developing 
input-output analysis, which uses linear algebra to model how 
industries depend on each other.

content.title: Simple economy
content.problem:
An economy has two sectors: manufacturing and energy. To produce 
\$1 of manufacturing output requires \$0.20 of manufacturing and 
\$0.30 of energy. To produce \$1 of energy requires \$0.40 of 
manufacturing and \$0.10 of energy. If external demand is \$50 
million for manufacturing and \$30 million for energy, what total 
output is needed from each sector?
content.solution:
Let $x_1$ = manufacturing output and $x_2$ = energy output (in millions).

The **consumption matrix** is:
\[
C = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 & 0.4 \\ 0.3 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix}
\]
Total output must satisfy:
\[
\vect{x} = C\vect{x} + \vect{d}
\]
where $\vect{d} = \begin{bmatrix} 50 \\ 30 \end{bmatrix}$ is external demand.

Rearranging: $(I - C)\vect{x} = \vect{d}$
\[
\begin{bmatrix} 0.8 & -0.4 \\ -0.3 & 0.9 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} = 
\begin{bmatrix} 50 \\ 30 \end{bmatrix}
\]
Row reducing the augmented matrix:
\[
\left[\begin{array}{cc|c}
0.8 & -0.4 & 50 \\
-0.3 & 0.9 & 30
\end{array}\right]
\to
\left[\begin{array}{cc|c}
1 & 0 & 100 \\
0 & 1 & 70
\end{array}\right]
\]
Manufacturing needs \$100 million output; energy needs \$70 million.

content.text:
Given $n$ data points, we can find a polynomial of degree $n-1$ 
passing through all of them by solving a linear system.

content.title: Fitting a parabola
content.problem:
Find the parabola $y = ax^2 + bx + c$ passing through the points 
$(1, 2)$, $(2, 3)$, and $(3, 6)$.
content.solution:
Substituting each point:
$$
a(1)^2 + b(1) + c &= 2 \\
a(2)^2 + b(2) + c &= 3 \\
a(3)^2 + b(3) + c &= 6
$$
This gives:
\[
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 1 & 1 \\
4 & 2 & 1 \\
9 & 3 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} a \\ b \\ c \end{bmatrix} = 
\begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \\ 6 \end{bmatrix}
\]
Row reducing: $a = 1$, $b = -2$, $c = 3$.

The parabola is $y = x^2 - 2x + 3$.

marginNotes.text:
What has been is what will be, and 
what has been done is what will be done, and there is nothing new 
under the sun.

marginNotes.author: Wassily Leontief (1905--1999)
marginNotes.text:
The purpose of the 
input-output model is to establish quantitative interdependence 
between the different sectors of production.
